items.q: Does brighter light carry more energy per photon?
items.correct: No — photon energy depends only on frequency (E = hf); greater intensity means more photons.
items.misconception: Yes — brighter light has more energetic photons.
items.random_wrong: Yes — intensity increases the speed of the photons.
items.why: Students conflate the total energy of a beam with the energy of each individual quantum.

items.q: When light slows down entering glass, does its frequency decrease?
items.correct: No — frequency is unchanged; the wavelength shortens as the speed drops.
items.misconception: Yes — slower light must oscillate more slowly.
items.random_wrong: Yes — glass absorbs half of the frequency.
items.why: A speed change suggests a frequency change, but frequency is fixed by the source; v = fλ adjusts via wavelength.

items.q: Below the threshold frequency, can very bright light release photoelectrons?
items.correct: No — each photon still has too little energy to overcome the work function, however many photons arrive.
items.misconception: Yes — enough intensity will eventually free the electrons.
items.random_wrong: Yes — after a short warming-up delay.
items.why: Wave thinking says energy accumulates, but photoemission is a one-photon, one-electron process.

items.q: E = hf tells us that a photon's energy is proportional to its:
items.why: Correct answer: Frequency. “Brightness” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Light entering glass from air bends towards the normal because:
items.correct: It slows down in the optically denser medium.
items.random_wrong: It speeds up in the glass.
items.why: It's It slows down in the optically denser medium.. “Gravity pulls the ray downwards.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The redshift of distant galaxies suggests that:
items.correct: They are receding from us, consistent with an expanding universe.
items.misconception: They are hotter than the Sun.
items.why: It's They are receding from us, consistent with an expanding universe.. “They are hotter than the Sun.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: According to Hubble's law, a galaxy twice as far away recedes:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Twice as fast. “Half as fast” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Using v = fλ, what is the frequency of a sound wave of wavelength 0.5 m travelling at 340 m/s?
items.why: The real figure is 680 Hz. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
